statement: After the 2013 Icelandic parliamentary elections, the new coalition government of the Progressive Party and Independence Party sought to withdraw Iceland's EU membership application. In February 2015, the governing parties submitted a bill to Althingi seeking approval to formally withdraw, but the bill failed due to vigorous opposition obstruction and was never put to a final vote. Foreign Minister Gunnar Bragi Sveinsson then unilaterally sent a letter to the EU on 12 March 2015, stating that Iceland should no longer be regarded as a candidate country. The EU's response was ambiguous — the European Commission's spokesperson stated that Sveinsson's letter did not constitute a formal withdrawal of the application. The Latvian EU presidency replied on behalf of the Council. The decision to bypass parliament provoked significant public protests outside the Althingi. The EU has continued to regard Iceland's 2009 application as technically still valid, a point that became relevant again in the lead-up to the 2026 referendum.
source_name: Icelandic Ministry for Foreign Affairs; EU Council of Ministers; Reykjavik Grapevine; IceNews
caveats: The legal status of Iceland's application remains disputed. The EU has never formally acknowledged the withdrawal, and the European Commission stated in March 2026 that the application is still valid. Icelandic legal scholars disagree on whether a foreign minister's letter can override a parliamentary process.

related_entries.statement: The current Icelandic government is a coalition of Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, 13 seats), Viðreisn (Reform Party, 11 seats), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party, 8 seats), holding 32 of 63 Althingi seats. The Prime Minister is Kristrún Frostadóttir (Samfylkingin). This is the first government coalition where both explicitly pro-EU parties (Samfylkingin and Viðreisn) serve together, which has been cited as a factor in advancing the EU referendum.

The Althingi (Icelandic parliament) has 63 seats. As of session 157, 57 seats are held by sitting MPs. The party breakdown is: Sjálfstæðisflokkur (Independence Party) 13, Samfylkingin (Social Democrats) 13, Viðreisn (Reform Party) 11, Miðflokkurinn (Centre Party) 8, Flokkur fólksins (People's Party) 8, Framsóknarflokkur (Progressive Party) 4. The remaining 6 seats are vacant or held by alternates (varamenn).

The coalition government formed in late 2024, comprising Samfylkingin (Social Democrats, 13 seats), Viðreisn (Reform Party, 11 seats), and Flokkur fólksins (People's Party, 8 seats), agreed in their coalition agreement to hold a referendum on whether to resume EU accession negotiations by 2027. The referendum was subsequently scheduled for 29 August 2026. The question asks whether Iceland should resume accession negotiations with the EU — not whether to join. This is the first Icelandic government since Jóhanna Sigurðardóttir's government (2009–2013) to actively pursue EU-related policy. Prime Minister Kristrún Frostadóttir (Samfylkingin) and Foreign Minister Þorgerður Katrín Gunnarsdóttir (Viðreisn) have been the primary government figures advancing the referendum. Previous governments, including those led by Sigmundur Davíð Gunnlaugsson (2013–2016) and Katrín Jakobsdóttir (2017–2024), did not pursue EU accession despite intermittent public calls for a referendum.

citations.report_title: 25. þáttur Silfursins: Stefnt að kosningu um ESB í sumar

claims.claim_text: Stjórnarandstöðuflokkarnir lofuðu að fara með ESB-málið áfram í þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu 2013 en gerðu það ekki og fóru ekki heldur með það fyrir utanríkismálanefnd.
Í yfir tíu ár hefur þjóðin verið klofin um ESB-málið eftir að kosturinn var tekinn af borðinu.

citations.report_title: Að kljúfa þjóðina með tilfinningaþrunginni spurningu

claims.claim_text: Fyrri ríkisstjórnir lofuðu þjóðaratkvæðagreiðslu um ESB-aðild en efndu ekki loforðið.

citations.report_title: Ekki víst hvernig Evrópusambandið bregst við

claims.claim_text: Deilt er um hvort aðildarumsókn Íslands frá 2010 sé enn í gildi.
Núverandi stjórnvöld halda því fram að aðildarumsóknin frá 2010 hafi aldrei verið formlega dregin til baka.
Spurningin um hvort þörf sé á ríkjaráðstefnu tengist beint spurningunni um hvort aðildarumsókn Íslands frá 2010 sé enn í gildi eða hvort leggja þurfi inn nýja umsókn.
